paragraphs.body: The Inevitable Relationship Between AI Scientists and Ethics Lately, there has been a brand new “automated research scientist Carl” published by the Autoscience Institute through the website AINEWS. The introduction of the new technology approach a new achievement in AI-based science, showing that artificial intelligence (AI) have developed the ability to write peer-reviewed papers. Although the launch of the novel autonomous AI researcher advanced humans’ exploration of the technology field, it sparks the ethical issues of AI in the domain of writing. In addition, Wiwanitmkit and Wiwanitkit (2024) mentioned that the “legitimacy and transparency of AI-generated content in scientific writing is one ethical concern.” It could be seen that the rising development of AI scientists would have a significant impact on the field of academic papers.

annotations.comment: Please use double spacing to format your paper.

paragraphs.body: While adding artificial intelligence to the scientific research field, AI-generated text was considered the main reason for raised concerns in science policy. The study performed by Hao et al. (2024) shows that the amount of paper published by individual scientists was 67.37 percent more while utilizing AI for support. Moreover, they get the team leader position 4 years sooner than others. The phenomenon has a high chance of lowering the engagement of in-depth research for scientists, which furthermore reduces the growth of data. They also found out from their study that “AI research spawns 24.40% less follow-on engagement, suggesting that AI papers tend to concentrate on extending the original papers, rather than forming densely interactions among each other” while analysing the likeness and frequency of the citing between the AI-generated content and the original paper. Additionally, the flattening of collective knowledge proves that “AI in science has become more concentrated around specific hot topics,” leading to “overlapping ideas and redundant innovations.” If the AI-based paper is less likely to bring up interactions of real researching, humans will end up suffering from shallow and fragmented research that lacks the depth of knowledge and innovation.

Although I concede that improvements in efficiency and productivity could be seen through the support of AI tools, I still maintain my position because the accuracy of AI-generated content is doubtful. A study was conducted by Khlaif et al. (2023), breaking out the potential concerns of using ChatGPT to generate scientific research. Via the prompts provided, ChatGPT came out with a research paper containing references. Throughout the study, it was discovered that the quality was unstable when comparing the different versions of paper generated by the AI. They conclude that it is weak in “developing studies”. Moreover, they found out that the percentage of references that were accessible on Google Scholar was as low as 8 percent. Hence, people should be more aware while working with AI for all the writing.

annotations.context_right: conclude that it is weak in

paragraphs.body: On the other hand, it turned out that numerous people use AI as a partner to peer review their articles or papers. Of course, it is valid to say that AI tools can support to reduce the misunderstandings of professional words or hard languages of the paper (Doskaliuk et al., 2025). However, it is risky to use AI with the existence of errors and biases. It might bring additional work since the generated paper is essential to be checked by humans. More and more regulations and cautions were posted. In order to maintain and ensure the “quality and credibility of public research”, editors were told to evaluate the use of AI due to the potential and possibility of including “bias and error from the chatbot”(Wiwanitmkit & Wiwanitkit, 2024). Noted by Chetwynd (2024), authors need to satisfy four criteria—proposed by the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors—to meet the requirement of authorship. Those criteria are “Substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data for the work”, “Drafting the work or reviewing it critically for important intellectual content”, “Final approval of the version to be published”, and “To be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.” As stated above, AI tools are not qualified to be listed as authors, so human authors are required to take final responsibility and ensure the validity of the content.

References Chetwynd, E. (2024). Ethical use of Artificial Intelligence for scientific writing: Current trends. Journal of Human Lactation, 40(2), 211–215. https://doi.org/10.1177/08903344241235160 Doskaliuk, B., Zimba, O., Yessirkepov, M., Klishch, I., & Yatsyshyn, R. (2025). Artificial Intelligence in peer review: Enhancing efficiency while preserving integrity. Journal of Korean Medical Science, 40(7). https://doi.org/10.3346/jkms.2025.40.e92 Hao, Q., Xu, F., Li, Y., & Evans, J. (2024, December 10). Ai expands scientists’ impact but contracts science’s focus. arXiv.org. https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.07727 Khlaif, Z. N., Mousa, A., Hattab, M. K., Itmazi, J., Hassan, A. A., Sanmugam, M., & Ayyoub, A. (2023). The potential and concerns of using AI in scientific research: CHATGPT Performance Evaluation. JMIR Medical Education, 9. https://doi.org/10.2196/47049 Wiwanitmkit, S., & Wiwanitkit, V. (2024). Artificial Intelligence, Academic Publishing, scientific writing, Peer Review, and Ethics. Brazilian Journal of Cardiovascular Surgery, 39(4). https://doi.org/10.21470/1678-9741-2023-0377

annotations.Peer Comment: Purpose/Thesis: After reading the draft, what would you say is the purpose or thesis the paper is trying to get across? How does the draft fulfill (or not fulfill) that purpose? The core claim of the paper is that although artificial intelligence has improved research efficiency, it has brought serious ethical issues, including accuracy, credibility, knowledge depth, and author responsibility. The author cited multiple studies to highlight the potential risks of AI-generated content to academic writing and argued that human researchers should still bear the primary responsibility. Persuasiveness: What did you learn about the topic after reading the paper that you didn’t fully appreciate before you read it? Is this something you find significant or interesting? If so, why? How well did the author address counterarguments? This article made me realize that AI is not just a writing tool, it will have a systematic impact on the overall development direction of academic research, citation network and knowledge depth. In particular, the points of "AI research reduces subsequent interactions" and "focuses on hot topics" are inspiring. I think this is a very meaningful research topic. Support: Did you find the claims, reasons, and evidence in the document convincing? Why or why not? Did you see a balanced coverage/an even distribution of claims and evidence? What information can be omitted or added to help your peer focus on the thesis? (Include paragraph numbers). The author provides data and opinions from multiple sources as support, and cites five academic papers, showing a strong research foundation. In particular, the research by Hao et al. and Khlaif et al. effectively supports the main argument. However, the data mentioned in paragraph 2 of "67.37%" and "24.40%" are specific, the explanation is slightly brief and further explanation can be added. Quoting and Paraphrasing: How effective has the author quoted and paraphrased information? Has the author used a signal phrase to introduce a quote/paraphrase and explain the quote/paraphrase in their own words? The citations are relatively standardized, and signal phrases are basically used. Organization: How is the draft organized in a logical manner (e.g., using headings/subheadings)? Given the organization, could the main points be presented in a more effective way? (Include paragraph numbers) The overall structure is clear, following the logic of "problem-impact-opposing viewpoint-conclusion", and the logic between paragraphs is basically coherent. There is no subheadings in the article. Paragraph 2 (analyzing how AI affects knowledge production) and Paragraph 3 (discussing content accuracy) are the core parts of the article.I think these two paragraphs are very effective ways of presenting. Transition: What suggestions can you make for transitions both between and within paragraphs that would make connections clearer and easier to follow? (Include paragraph numbers). The overall paragraphs are connected naturally and logically. The connection from the end of the fourth paragraph to the end can also be closer, for example, by adding a summary sentence to respond to the proposition of the entire text. The sentences: Choose 1-2 sentences you consider the most interesting or best written—stylistically effective, entertaining, or otherwise memorable. Then choose 1-2 sentences you see as weak—confusing, awkward, or uninspiring. Advise your peer on how to revise the weak sentences. (Include paragraph numbers) “If the AI-based paper is less likely to bring up interactions of real researching, humans will end up suffering from shallow and fragmented research that lacks the depth of knowledge and innovation.” This sentence accurately summarizes the impact of AI on the depth of research.( Paragraph 2 ) “Hence, people should be more aware while working with AI for all the writing.” hence may be a bit colloquial, so it is recommended to be revised to “Therefore, users must exercise greater caution when relying on AI-generated content in academic writing.”（Paragraph 3） The format/design: To the best of your ability, check for and point out mechanical/grammatical errors, word choice issues, spelling mistakes, and APA formatting issues. Did the authors use formal academic language and a first/third person serious tone? (Include paragraph numbers). The overall language style is formal, using the third person and academic tone, which basically conforms to the APA writing style. Introduction/conclusion: What strategy/strategies have been used to introduce and conclude the draft? Does the introduction catch the reader’s interest? Does the draft conclude in a memorable way? How else might this draft begin and end? The introduction starts with a case study, which arouses the reader's interest and further expands on the background of the rapid rise of AI in academic writing. The ending is relatively concise, so consider adding a little more. Any other comments? Nothing else.
